title: Why Intuition Is Often Suppressed in Childhood
meta_description: Discover why intuition is often suppressed in childhood. Learn how early corrections and quiet dismissals shape your inner voice.
og_title: Why Intuition Is Often Suppressed in Childhood
og_description: Discover why intuition is often suppressed in childhood. Learn how early corrections and quiet dismissals shape your inner voice.
body:
You Were Born Knowing. Then You Were Taught Not To.

There's a memory I carry from when I was about six years old. I was sitting at the kitchen table, and my mother was telling me about her friend Linda. I didn't know much about Linda, but I felt a tightness in my chest when her name came up. A cold, sharp twist. I said, "Mom, I don't think Linda is a good person." My mother stopped mid-sentence and looked at me funny. "Don't say that," she said. "You don't even know her."

I didn't say anything else. But I knew. I knew what I felt was real. And I also learned something that day: what I knew wasn't welcome.

That's how it starts. Not with a bang, but with a quiet correction. A look. A sigh. A "don't be silly." And over time, you stop trusting the thing that was once as natural as breathing: your intuition.

If you're here, reading this, it's probably because you've felt that. You've felt the weight of being told your gut feelings were wrong, dramatic, or "too much." And you've spent years trying to unlearn that message. Let's talk about why this happens, how it shapes you, and what you can do about it. Right?

The Bright Wound: What It Means to Be a Sensitive Kid

I call it the Bright Wound. It's the name for what happens when a person who feels deeply, senses clearly, and picks up on things others don't -- gets told, over and over, that their experience isn't real. The wound is bright because it's made of light. Your sensitivity is a gift. But it gets wrapped in shame, confusion, and doubt. And that's the wound.

Think about it. You're a kid. You walk into a room and feel a heavy, sticky energy between your parents. They're not fighting. They're not even talking. But you feel it. You feel the tension like a fog. So you say something. "Are you guys okay?" And they look at you like you're crazy. "We're fine," they say. But they're not fine. And you know they're not fine. But they said they are. So who do you believe? Them? Or the feeling in your gut?

Most kids learn to believe the adults. Because adults are bigger. Adults are in charge. Adults have the words. And you're just a kid who feels too much. So you start to think there's something wrong with you. You start to think your feelings are wrong. You start to shut down the part of you that knows.

That's the Bright Wound. And it's more common than you think.

"The wound is bright because it's made of light. Your sensitivity is a gift. But it gets wrapped in shame, confusion, and doubt. And that's the wound."

Why Adults Shut Down Your Intuition (It's Not About You)

Here's the thing that took me years to understand: adults don't shut down your intuition because they're mean. They do it because they're scared. Most adults are walking around with their own intuition buried six feet under. They've learned to ignore their own gut feelings. So when you come along and say, "I feel something weird about Uncle Bob," it's not just inconvenient. It's threatening. It reminds them of what they've lost. Or worse, it makes them uncomfortable because they feel it too, but they don't want to admit it.

So they tell you to stop. They tell you you're imagining things. They tell you to be rational. To use your head, not your heart. To stop being so sensitive. They're not trying to hurt you. They're trying to protect themselves. But the result is the same: you learn to doubt yourself.

There's also the practical side. Intuition is messy. It doesn't follow rules. It doesn't care about schedules or social niceties. If you're a parent trying to get your kid to school on time, you don't want them stopping to say, "I feel a bad energy in this car." You want them to put on their shoes and get in the car. So you train them to ignore the subtle signals. And slowly, the volume on their inner knowing gets turned down.

Does that land? I hope so. Because understanding why it happens is the first step to unhooking from the shame. It wasn't your fault. You were just a kid with a gift that nobody knew how to handle.

The Three Main Ways Intuition Gets Suppressed in Childhood

Let's break this down into the specific patterns I see over and over in the people I work with. These are the three main ways the Bright Wound gets created.

1. The Direct Dismissal: "That's Not Real"

This is the most obvious one. You say something you know, and you're told you're wrong. Maybe it's a feeling about a person, a place, or a situation. Maybe it's a dream that feels significant. Maybe it's a knowing that something bad is going to happen. And the adult in your life says, "Don't be silly," or "You're just imagining things," or "That's not possible."

This is like someone telling you the sky is green when you can see it's blue. Over time, you start to question your own eyes. You start to think maybe you are making it up. You start to believe that your inner world isn't trustworthy. This is the most direct way intuition gets suppressed, and it's also the most damaging because it makes you doubt the very foundation of your own experience.

For a kid who is highly intuitive, this can feel like gaslighting. Because it is. Not malicious gaslighting, but gaslighting nonetheless. You're being told your reality isn't real. And that's a hard thing to recover from.

2. The Emotional Shutdown: "Stop Being So Sensitive"

This one is more subtle. It's not about what you say you know. It's about how you feel. Intuitive kids don't just pick up on information. They pick up on energy. They feel the emotions of the people around them. They walk into a room and absorb the mood like a sponge. And that can be overwhelming.

When you say, "I feel sad," and the adult says, "You have nothing to be sad about," you learn that your feelings are wrong. When you say, "I feel scared," and the adult says, "Don't be a baby," you learn that fear is unacceptable. When you say, "I feel angry," and the adult says, "Don't talk to me like that," you learn that anger is dangerous.

So you start to shut down your feelings. You start to numb out. You start to disconnect from your body. And since intuition speaks through feelings and body sensations, you're effectively cutting off the communication line. You can't hear your intuition if you can't feel your feelings. Know what I mean?

3. The Over-Intellectualization: "Use Your Head, Not Your Heart"

This is the one that gets praised. The kid who is "so rational," "so logical," "so mature for their age." This is the kid who learned early that feelings are messy and thinking is safe. So they become a thinker. They analyze everything. They make lists. They weigh pros and cons. They never make a decision without a spreadsheet.

And on the surface, this looks like success. But underneath, it's a survival strategy. The intuitive part of them got shut down so thoroughly that they built a whole fortress of logic to keep it from leaking out. They're not using their head because it's better. They're using their head because they're terrified of what they might feel if they stopped.

This is especially common in boys, who are often socialized to be "tough" and "rational." But it happens to girls too. Anyone who was rewarded for being smart and punished for being emotional can end up in this camp. And the tragedy is, they often don't even know what they're missing. They think they're fine. They think they're successful. But there's a hollow feeling inside. A sense that something is missing. And that's their intuition, knocking on the door, trying to get back in.

How Suppressed Intuition Shows Up in Adult Life

So what happens when you grow up with a suppressed intuition? You don't just lose your psychic abilities. You lose your sense of self. Here's what it looks like in practice.

You second-guess everything. You make a decision, then immediately wonder if it was the right one. You ask everyone for their opinion before you trust your own. You stay in bad relationships because you can't tell if your gut is telling you to leave or if you're just being dramatic. You take jobs that drain you because you talked yourself out of the feeling that something was off.

You also struggle with anxiety. Think about it. If your intuition is a guidance system, and you've learned to ignore it, you're flying blind. Of course you're anxious. You don't know what's coming. You don't know who to trust. You don't know what you want. You're navigating life without your internal compass. That's exhausting.

And you might also feel a deep loneliness. Because the part of you that connects to others, that senses their emotions, that knows when they're hurting or happy -- that part is buried. You can't connect deeply with others if you can't connect deeply with yourself. And you can't connect deeply with yourself if you've shut down your intuition.

If any of this sounds familiar, I want you to know something: you're not broken. You're not too sensitive. You're not crazy. You're just a person who was taught to doubt the very thing that makes you powerful. And that can be unlearned.

Reclaiming Your Intuition: Where to Start

So how do you start to turn the volume back up? How do you start to trust that inner knowing again? It's not about becoming a psychic overnight. It's about small, consistent acts of trust. Here are a few places to start.

Start With Your Body

Your intuition lives in your body. Not in your head. So the first step is to get out of your head and into your body. This can be as simple as taking three deep breaths and noticing what you feel. Where is there tension? Where is there ease? What is your gut telling you right now?

Don't try to interpret it. Don't analyze it. Just notice it. The more you practice this, the more you'll start to recognize the physical signals of your intuition. A tight chest. A warm feeling in your stomach. A sudden urge to move. These are all clues.

Start Small With Trust

Don't try to make a huge life decision based on intuition right away. Start with small things. What do you want for breakfast? Don't think about it. Just feel it. Which way do you want to walk? Don't plan it. Just go with the feeling. Who do you want to call? Don't overthink it. Just notice the impulse.

Every time you follow a small intuitive nudge and it works out, you're building trust. You're rewiring the pattern that says your inner knowing can't be trusted. Over time, those small wins add up. And you'll find yourself making bigger decisions with more confidence.

Give Yourself Permission to Be Wrong

Here's the thing about intuition: it's not always right. Sometimes you'll get a feeling that doesn't pan out. That's okay. It doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you're human. The goal isn't to be perfect. The goal is to be in conversation with your inner knowing.

If you need to be right every time, you'll never take the risk of trusting. So give yourself permission to be wrong. Let yourself make mistakes. That's how you learn the difference between fear and intuition, between anxiety and knowing, between wishful thinking and genuine guidance.

Books That Can Help You Reconnect

If you want to go deeper, there are some excellent books that can support you on this journey. These are resources I recommend to people who are ready to start reclaiming their intuitive gifts.

One of my favorites is The Intuitive Way: A Guide to Living from Inner Wisdom by Penney Peirce. This book is a practical, grounded guide to understanding and developing your intuition. It's not woo-woo or abstract. It's direct and usable. You can see it on Amazon (paid link) if you want to check it out.

Another powerful resource is The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. This book is about listening to your survival instincts, which are a form of intuition. It's particularly helpful if you've been taught to override your gut feelings about people and situations. It's a wake-up call. You can see it on Amazon (paid link).

And if you're ready to go all in on understanding your sensitivity as a gift, I highly recommend The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. This book changed my life. It helped me understand that my sensitivity wasn't a flaw, it was a trait. And that there are millions of people just like me. You can see it on Amazon (paid link).

What Your Intuition Actually Is (And Isn't)

Let's clear something up. Intuition isn't magic. It's not a superpower. It's not about predicting the lottery or knowing who's calling before you look at your phone (though that can happen). Intuition is simply your subconscious mind processing information faster than your conscious mind can keep up with. It's pattern recognition. It's your body's wisdom. It's the part of you that knows things before you have words for them.

When you were a kid, that part of you was wide open. You hadn't learned to filter it out yet. But over time, you were taught to ignore it. To doubt it. To dismiss it. And now, as an adult, you have to consciously choose to listen again.

The good news is, it's never too late. Your intuition is still there. It's been waiting for you. It hasn't gone anywhere. It's just been buried under years of conditioning. But with patience, practice, and a willingness to trust yourself, you can dig it back up.

You're Not Too Much. You're Just Right.

I want to leave you with this. If you grew up being told you were too sensitive, too emotional, too dramatic, too intuitive, too much -- I'm here to tell you that you're not too much. You're exactly the right amount. The people around you just didn't have the capacity to hold what you were offering.

Your intuition is not a flaw. It's a gift. And the world needs people like you who can feel the truth beneath the surface. Who can sense when something is off. Who can connect to the invisible currents of energy that move through all of us.

So take a breath. Put your hand on your heart. And ask yourself: what do I know right now, in this moment, that I've been ignoring? Don't think about it. Just feel it. The answer is already there. It's always been there.

If you're ready to go deeper and start actively rebuilding your intuitive trust, I've put together a free quiz that can help you identify where you are on your journey. It's quick, it's honest, and it might give you some clarity. Take the Intuition Style Quiz here to see how your childhood conditioning is still affecting your decision-making today.
